edges.description: Expanded polyglutamine proteins misfold and aggregate, overwhelming neuronal protein-quality-control systems.
edges.source: Repeat Expansion and Polyglutamine Toxic Gain-of-Function
edges.target: Misfolded Protein Aggregation and Proteostatic Stress

edges.description: Expanded ATXN1 and TBP perturb transcriptional regulatory complexes in vulnerable neurons.
edges.source: Repeat Expansion and Polyglutamine Toxic Gain-of-Function

edges.description: Chronic proteostatic stress and aggregate burden drive progressive Purkinje cell dysfunction and loss.
edges.source: Misfolded Protein Aggregation and Proteostatic Stress
edges.target: Purkinje Cell Degeneration and Cerebellar Cortical Atrophy

edges.description: The same misfolded-protein toxicity acts in parallel on brainstem, pyramidal-tract, and peripheral neuron populations, producing extracerebellar degeneration independently of (rather than secondary to) Purkinje-cell loss.
edges.source: Misfolded Protein Aggregation and Proteostatic Stress

edges.description: Sustained transcriptional dysregulation compromises Purkinje-cell survival programs.
edges.target: Purkinje Cell Degeneration and Cerebellar Cortical Atrophy

edges.description: Transcriptional dysregulation acts on the same vulnerable extracerebellar neuron populations (brainstem, pyramidal-tract, and peripheral neurons), contributing in parallel to their degeneration.
Purkinje-cell loss disrupts cerebellar motor coordination, producing the progressive gait and limb ataxia that define the syndrome.
edges.source: Purkinje Cell Degeneration and Cerebellar Cortical Atrophy

edges.description: Cerebellar cortical degeneration impairs speech coordination and causes cerebellar dysarthria.
edges.source: Purkinje Cell Degeneration and Cerebellar Cortical Atrophy

edges.description: Degeneration of extracerebellar corticospinal/pyramidal systems produces pyramidal tract signs.
Basal-ganglia and other extracerebellar motor-system involvement produces extrapyramidal movement abnormalities.
Extracerebellar cortical-network involvement contributes to cognitive impairment in affected subtypes.
Involvement of peripheral sensory and motor neurons produces peripheral neuropathy in multiple ADCA-I-associated SCA subtypes.
Brainstem and ocular-motor circuit involvement in SCA2 produces slow saccades.
intermediate_mechanisms: brainstem and ocular-motor network involvement in SCA2

nodes.description: Pyramidal tract signs are extracerebellar features that distinguish ADCA-I from the predominantly pure-cerebellar ADCA-III class.

evidence.reference_title: The wide spectrum of spinocerebellar ataxias (SCAs).
evidence.snippet: They can present also with pigmentary retinopathy, extrapyramidal movement disorders (parkinsonism, dyskinesias, dystonia, chorea), pyramidal signs, cortical symptoms (seizures, cognitive impairment/behavioral symptoms), peripheral neuropathy.

nodes.description: Extrapyramidal movement-disorder features such as parkinsonism, dystonia, chorea, and dyskinesia occur in ADCA-I subtypes, especially SCA17.

evidence.reference_title: Complexity of the Genetics and Clinical Presentation of Spinocerebellar Ataxia 17.
evidence.snippet: SCA17 is characterized by ataxia, pyramidal and extrapyramidal signs, cognitive impairments, psychosis, and seizures as well as involuntary movements including chorea and dystonia

nodes.description: Cognitive impairment or behavioral symptoms occur in several ADCA-I subtypes as extracerebellar cortical-network involvement.

evidence.reference_title: Complexity of the Genetics and Clinical Presentation of Spinocerebellar Ataxia 17.
evidence.snippet: SCA17 is characterized by ataxia, pyramidal and extrapyramidal signs, cognitive impairments, psychosis, and seizures as well as involuntary movements including chorea and dystonia

nodes.description: Scanning/cerebellar dysarthria accompanies the ataxic syndrome across ADCA-I subtypes as part of the progressive cerebellar degeneration.

evidence.reference_title: The wide spectrum of spinocerebellar ataxias (SCAs).
evidence.snippet: Patients exhibit usually a slowly progressive cerebellar syndrome with various combinations of oculomotor disorders, dysarthria, dysmetria/kinetic tremor, and/or ataxic gait.

nodes.description: The defining distinction of ADCA-I from the pure-cerebellar ADCA-III class is involvement of extracerebellar systems: brainstem and pontine nuclei, the pyramidal tracts, basal ganglia (extrapyramidal signs), peripheral sensory and motor nerves, and — in some subtypes — cognitive networks. The specific extracerebellar burden varies by subtype (e.g., motor-neuron involvement in SCA36; cognitive/psychiatric and extrapyramidal features in SCA17; peripheral neuropathy in SCA2/SCA4).

evidence.reference_title: Complexity of the Genetics and Clinical Presentation of Spinocerebellar Ataxia 17.
evidence.snippet: SCA17 is characterized by ataxia, pyramidal and extrapyramidal signs, cognitive impairments, psychosis, and seizures as well as involuntary movements including chorea and dystonia

nodes.description: Expanded polyglutamine ataxin-1, ataxin-2, and TBP adopt aberrant conformations and form nuclear and cytoplasmic inclusions. Sequestration of chaperones, ubiquitin-proteasome and autophagy components, and interacting partners progressively impairs neuronal proteostasis, contributing to Purkinje-cell and brainstem-neuron dysfunction. This proteostatic stress is a convergent downstream consequence of the polyglutamine subtypes and is described mechanistically here without an independent ADCA-I-specific quantitative claim.
nodes.id: Misfolded Protein Aggregation and Proteostatic Stress

evidence.snippet: the dramatic effects of altering protein conformations on neuronal function and survival

nodes.description: Peripheral neuropathy is part of the extracerebellar burden in several ADCA-I subtypes, including SCA1, SCA2, SCA3, SCA4, SCA8, SCA18, and SCA25.

evidence.reference_title: The wide spectrum of spinocerebellar ataxias (SCAs).
evidence.snippet: peripheral neuropathy in SCA1, SCA2, SCA3, SCA4, SCA8, SCA18 and SCA25.

nodes.description: Progressive gait and limb ataxia from cerebellar Purkinje-cell degeneration is the core, defining clinical feature shared by all ADCA-I subtypes. Subtype-level frequency and severity detail is deferred to the individual subtype entries (scaffold stage).

evidence.reference_title: The wide spectrum of spinocerebellar ataxias (SCAs).
evidence.snippet: Patients exhibit usually a slowly progressive cerebellar syndrome with various combinations of oculomotor disorders, dysarthria, dysmetria/kinetic tremor, and/or ataxic gait.

nodes.description: The convergent pathological endpoint across ADCA-I subtypes is progressive degeneration of cerebellar Purkinje cells with loss of the cerebellar cortical architecture, producing the progressive gait and limb ataxia, dysarthria, and oculomotor abnormalities that define the syndrome.
nodes.id: Purkinje Cell Degeneration and Cerebellar Cortical Atrophy

evidence.reference_title: Complexity of the Genetics and Clinical Presentation of Spinocerebellar Ataxia 17.
evidence.snippet: Brain MRI shows variable atrophy of the cerebral cortex, brain stem, and cerebellum

nodes.description: The unifying molecular lesion across ADCA-I subtypes is an unstable expanded repeat that confers a toxic gain-of-function. In the polyglutamine subtypes (SCA1/ATXN1, SCA2/ATXN2, SCA17/TBP) a CAG expansion encodes an elongated polyglutamine tract; in SCA8 a bidirectionally transcribed CTG/CAG expansion combines RNA toxicity with a polyglutamine product; and in SCA36 a non-coding GGCCTG hexanucleotide expansion drives RNA-mediated toxicity. Repeat instability during meiosis underlies anticipation and the inverse correlation between repeat length and age at onset.
nodes.id: Repeat Expansion and Polyglutamine Toxic Gain-of-Function

evidence.snippet: The discovery that expansion of unstable repeats can cause a variety of neurological disorders has changed the landscape of disease-oriented research for several forms of mental retardation, Huntington disease, inherited ataxias, and muscular dystrophy.

evidence.reference_title: Complexity of the Genetics and Clinical Presentation of Spinocerebellar Ataxia 17.
evidence.snippet: Spinocerebellar ataxia type 17 (SCA17) is a rare autosomal dominant neurodegenerative disease caused by a CAG repeat expansion in the TATA-box binding protein gene (TBP).

nodes.description: Slow saccades are a characteristic oculomotor manifestation of SCA2 and contribute to the extracerebellar eye-movement phenotype in ADCA-I.

evidence.reference_title: The wide spectrum of spinocerebellar ataxias (SCAs).

nodes.description: Several ADCA-I proteins act in the nucleus: TBP is a core general transcription factor and expanded ataxin-1 reorganizes native transcriptional regulator complexes (e.g., with CIC and RBM17), skewing gene-expression programs required for Purkinje-cell homeostasis. Transcriptional dysregulation is a shared nuclear mechanism among the polyglutamine ADCA-I subtypes; subtype-specific molecular detail is left for follow-up curation of the individual subtype entries.

biological_processes: regulation of transcription by RNA polymerase II

evidence.reference_title: Complexity of the Genetics and Clinical Presentation of Spinocerebellar Ataxia 17.
evidence.snippet: neurodegenerative disease caused by a CAG repeat expansion in the TATA-box binding protein gene (TBP)
